perturbation.original_text: "Trade Secrets" means all information, in any form or medium, to the extent that the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret and the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, the public.
perturbation.changed_text: "Trade Secrets" means all information, in any form or medium, regardless of the measures taken to keep such information secret, and regardless of whether the information derives independent economic value from not being generally known to the public.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that the owner must take 'reasonable measures to keep such information secret' and that the information must derive independent economic value, the definition now contradicts the established legal definition of trade secrets. This introduces ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of trade secret protections within the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) and corresponding state laws define trade secrets as information that the owner has taken reasonable measures to keep secret and that derives independent economic value from not being generally known.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition removes the 'reasonable measures' and 'independent economic value' criteria. This contradicts 18 U.S. Code § 1839, which requires these elements for information to qualify as a trade secret. Without these elements, information lacks legal protection under trade secret law.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for 'reasonable measures to keep such information secret' and 'independent economic value' for something to qualify as a trade secret. 18 U.S. Code § 1839, as referenced in the Law Explanation, requires these elements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referring to 18 USC 1839, shows that this statute defines terms related to trade secret protection, and the definition of 'trade secret' therein includes the components removed in the changed text, making the modified text a contradiction of the law.

perturbation.original_text: "Patents" means patents (including all reissues, divisionals, continuations, continuations-in-part, reexaminations, supplemental examinations, inter partes review, post-grant oppositions, covered business methods reviews, substitutions and extensions thereof), patent registrations and applications, including provisional applications, statutory invention registrations, invention disclosures and inventions.
perturbation.changed_text: "Patents" means patents (excluding all reissues, divisionals, continuations, continuations-in-part, reexaminations, supplemental examinations, inter partes review, post-grant oppositions, covered business methods reviews, substitutions and extensions thereof), patent registrations and applications, including provisional applications, statutory invention registrations, invention disclosures and inventions.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding reissues, divisionals, continuations, continuations-in-part, reexaminations, supplemental examinations, inter partes review, post-grant oppositions, covered business methods reviews, substitutions and extensions from the definition of 'Patents,' the contract now contradicts standard patent law practices where these are considered part of the patent family and protection. This change creates uncertainty about the scope of the license grants and other provisions related to patents.
perturbation.contradicted_law: United States patent law recognizes continuations, divisionals, reissues, and re-examinations as part of the overall patent protection system and related to the original patent.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original definition of 'Patents' aligns with standard legal understanding, whereas the modified version attempts to exclude standard patent-related processes. This contradicts 35 U.S. Code § 120 (Continuity) and 35 U.S. Code § 251 (Reissue), which define these procedures as legitimate parts of the patent system. By excluding them, the modified definition attempts to limit the scope of patent protection in a way that conflicts with established patent law.

Benefit of earlier filing date in the United States An application for patent for an invention disclosed in the manner provided by section 112(a) (other than the requirement to disclose the best mode) in an application previously filed in the United States, or as provided by section 363 or 385, which names an inventor or joint inventor in the previously filed applica
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 120 - Benefit of earlier filing date in the United States | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 120 - Benefit of earlier filing date in the United States U.S. Code Notes prev | next An application for patent for an invention disclosed in the manner provided by section 112(a) (other than the requirement to disclose the best mode) in an application previously filed in the United States, or as provided by section 363 or 385, which names an inventor or joint inventor in the previously filed application shall have the same effect, as to such invention, as though filed on the date of the prior application, if filed before the patenting or abandonment of or termination of proceedings on the first application or on an application similarly entitled to the benefit of the filing date of the first application and if it contains or is amended to contain a specific reference to the earlier filed application. No application shall be entitled to the benefit of an earlier filed application under this section unless an amendment containing the specific reference to the earlier filed application is submitted at such time during the pendency of the application as required by the Director. The Director may consider the failure to submit such an amendment within that time period as a waiver of any benefit under this section. The Director may establish procedures, including the requirement for payment of the fee specified in section 41(a)(7), to accept an unintentionally delayed submission of an amendment under this section. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 800 ; Pub. L. 94–131, § 9 , Nov. 14, 1975 , 89 Stat. 691 ; Pub. L. 98–622, title I, § 104(b) , Nov. 8, 1984 , 98 Stat. 3385 ; Pub. L. 106–113, div. B, § 1000(a)(9) [title IV, § 4503(b)(1)] , Nov. 29, 1999 , 113 Stat. 1536 , 1501A–563; Pub. L. 112–29 , §§ 3(f), 15(b), 20(j), Sept. 16, 2011 , 125 Stat. 288 , 328, 3
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text explicitly excludes 'reissues, divisionals, continuations, continuations-in-part, reexaminations, supplemental examinations, inter partes review, post-grant oppositions, covered business methods reviews, substitutions and extensions thereof' from the definition of 'Patents.' This contradicts the legal understanding of patent law, which includes these processes as part of the patent system. As evidenced by the LLM's 'Law Explanation' and the definition of 'Benefit of earlier filing date in the United States' in 35 U.S. Code § 120 found in both law snippets, processes such as continuations are considered legitimate parts of the patent system. By excluding them, the modified definition limits the scope of patent protection contrary to established patent law.

perturbation.original_text: "Copyrights" means copyrights, works of authorship (including all translations, adaptations, derivations and combinations thereof), mask works, designs and database rights, including, in each case, any registrations and applications therefor.
perturbation.changed_text: "Copyrights" means copyrights, works of authorship (excluding all translations, adaptations, derivations and combinations thereof), mask works, designs and database rights, excluding any registrations and applications therefor.
perturbation.explanation: By excluding translations, adaptations, derivations and combinations thereof, as well as registrations and applications, this definition conflicts with the scope of copyright protection afforded under copyright law. Removing registrations from the definition means that unregistered copyrights would be the only things covered, which creates uncertainty regarding enforceability of copyrights in jurisdictions that require registration.
perturbation.contradicted_law: The Copyright Act of 1976 grants copyright protection to original works of authorship, including derivative works, and provides additional rights and remedies for registered copyrights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified definition attempts to exclude derivative works (translations, adaptations, etc.) and registered copyrights, conflicting with 17 U.S. Code § 103 (Subject matter of copyright: Compilations and derivative works) and 17 U.S. Code § 408 (Copyright registration in general). This is an inaccurate representation of copyright law and would create enforceability issues.

Subject matter of copyright: Compilations and derivative works (a) The subject matter of copyright as specified by section 102 includes compilations and derivative works, but protection for a work employing preexisting material in which copyright subsists does not extend to any part of the work in which such material has been used unlawfully. (b) The copyright in a compilation or derivative work extends only to the material contribute
perturbation.justification: The changed text's exclusion of "translations, adaptations, derivations and combinations thereof" from the definition of "Copyrights" directly contradicts 17 U.S. Code § 103, which states that the subject matter of copyright includes compilations and derivative works. The changed text also excludes registrations, creating uncertainty about enforceability, further undermining copyright law. The provided snippet directly supports the conclusion that excluding derivative works creates a legal contradiction. The change removes core legal protections afforded to copyright holders.
